text: The laparoscopic setup for the Ladd's procedure is shown, with the infant positioned at the foot of the table. Trocar placement includes a 4 or 5 millimeter port in the umbilicus, and right and left hand operating ports positioned on either side of the umbilicus. In this small infant, the right hand port is placed above the umbilicus to avoid interference with the laparoscopic scope.

The surgeon performs initial dissection under the liver, revealing a very dilated proximal duodenum. Adhesions and omentum are carefully dissected and sealed off using a three millimeter sealer, which also functions as atraumatic forceps. The duodenum is followed distally, with all attachments being taken down to fully mobilize it. The first portion of the duodenum appears extremely dilated and tortuous, with multiple dense adhesions safely dissected using the sealer. The bowel is grasped immediately after sealing without signs of heat injury.

The surgeon continues the dissection in a proximal to distal fashion, exposing the second portion of the duodenum. Dense adhesions around the duodenum are visible, and upward pressure is applied to the free portion of the duodenum to expose and release additional adhesions, aiming to completely free the tortuous duodenum.

The surgeon continues to free up the duodenum, revealing the caudate lobe of the liver. The duodenum is seen extending towards the retroperitoneum, with visible attachments from the transverse colon to the second and third portions of the duodenum. These adhesions, including thick and dense LAD bands, are being carefully dissected.

The surgeon uses the sealer to atraumatically dissect adhesions off the duodenum, focusing on posterior attachments. The transverse and proximal colon are mobilized, revealing stretched lateral wall attachments between the cecum and retroperitoneum, which are easily taken down. The mobilization of LADS bands allows the colon to flip into the left abdomen, exposing tortuous duodenum and LADS bands crossing the ileum, causing partial obstruction.

The surgeon uses a sealer device with minimal energy spread to safely dissect the last of the large LADS bands traversing across the duodenum, which was causing proximal obstruction. The duodenum is seen doubling back on itself. The final attachments are mobilized, allowing the colon to be repositioned into the left portion of the abdomen, and the kinking retroperitoneal attachment of the duodenum is taken down.

The surgeon visualizes the posterior aspect of the duodenum, which is now completely mobilized. The freed duodenum is grasped and pulled down to straighten it, revealing the transition area where the proximal portion is extremely dilated. The bowel is run from proximal to distal to ensure complete derotation, eliminating the risk of volvulus.

The surgeon uses a sealer as an atraumatic bowel grasper to safely seal and divide encountered bands. The bowel is run until the terminal ileum and cecum are reached, showing the bowel almost completely derotated. Enlarged lymph nodes in the mesentery and a chylous appearance within the bowel indicate chronic mild obstruction.

The surgeon mobilizes the mesentery of the appendix using a sealer, preparing for its removal. The colon is positioned on the left side of the abdomen, while the small bowel is on the right, indicating completion of the Ladd's procedure. The appendix is either removed intracorporeally or, in the case of a small infant, brought out through the right three-millimeter trocar site for extracorporeal amputation.
